The impact of the Prandtl number on the temperature profile (0.00624 ≤ Pr ≤ 16) (a) Comparison of mean temperature profiles obtained from DNS of Pirozzoli [229] (solid lines), with the predictions of Eq. (22) (dashed lines) and with Kader's empirical fit Eq. (20) (circles). (b) A magnified view to better observe the behaviour of the low-Pr profiles. The figure is adopted from Pirozzoli [229].

(a) Schematic representation of projected surface area (A f ), plane surface area (A p ), and total plane area (A t ), used to define frontal and plane solidity. (b)-(f) Schematic of positive skewness (b), negative skewness (c), Gaussian roughness (d), leptokurtic (e), and Platykurtic (f).

Left: a segment of the simulated channel displaying instantaneous temperature contours. Middle: instantaneous velocity vectors near a recirculation zone from the frame F 1, with velocity vectors not to scale. Right: time-averaged temperature contours near a recirculation zone from the frame F 2. The figure is adopted from Peeters & Sandham [61].
Schematic illustration of the horseshoe and hairpin vortices over a hemispherical roughness, re-generated from Acarlar & Smith [331] by permission of Cambridge University Press.

(a) Schematic of the fully rough model representing the Prandtl–Blasius-type laminar boundary layers cover the entire rough surface. (b) Schematic illustration of the case where the Pr ≈ 1, resulting in the thermal boundary layer thickness δ θ ∝ δ v Pr m − 1 = δ v Pr 1/3, where δ θ and δ v are the thickness of the CSL and VSL, respectively. (c) Example solutions from Prandtl–Blasius theory depict velocity (black) and temperature (red) profiles for Pr = 5.0. The linear VSL and CSL regions, δ θ and δ v , respectively, are indicated by the minima of their second derivatives (marked by square markers). The original figure is adopted from Zhong et al. [4] and modified by the authors.

Comparison of various correlations in calculating the inverse roughness Stanton number. The results of correlations are compared with the DNS data of Kuwata [63] for (Pr =1.0), LES data of Garg et al. [92,94] for (Pr = 0.7), and experimental data of Dipprey & Sabersky [297] (Pr = 1.2). The correlations are evaluated at Pr = 1.0

(a) Schematic of the surface renewal (Kolmogorov–Brutsaert) model based on Kolmogorov energy cascading phenomenology. Roughness creates eddies with a size proportional to k and a new scale separation between the roughness-scale eddies and the Kolmogorov eddies with size η, scouring the surface area. The cascading is characterised by the rate of turbulent energy dissipation ε = u τ 3/κk. (b) Ensembled Kolmogorov eddies initially carry temperature Θ i from the well-mixed region. (c) probability density function (p.d.f) of ensembled Kolmogorov eddies. The red region is the CSL. The original figure is adopted from Zhong et al. [4] and modified by the authors.
